entry.title: The SAP Extended Warehouse Management FAQ Series: SCM Optimizer for EWM
entry.content:
With the introduction of Unified Package Building (UPB) in Extended Warehouse Management for SAP S/4HANA, many of our customers would like to understand more about how they could use the SCM Optimizer in their implementation projects and what are the aspects to consider.

We collected some interesting facts and typical questions on SCM Optimizer usage for EWM in the following blog post.

What is the SCM Optimizer?

The SCM Optimizer is a software (implemented in C/C++) that can be installed on the application server or on a separate service running on Windows or Linux. The SCM Optimizer, also called “Optimizer”, can be used by SAP backend solutions, such as Transportation Management or Extended Warehouse Management, for algorithms-based problem solving.

The software component SCM Optimizer contains a collection of different optimizer engines. Each engine is delivered as a separate executable inside the archive. The engines are specialized on different kinds of optimization scenarios, e.g. scheduling or network planning, pallet building and more.

Find more information about the SCM Optimizer via SAP Note 1640509 - FAQ about the setup of SCM Optimizer.

Where is the SCM Optimizer used in EWM for SAP S/4HANA Private Cloud Edition (PCE)?

Since SAP EWM 9.5, the SCM Optimizer (VSO engine) can be used in EWM through the Unified Package Build, enabling the integration of Package Building (PB) for packing in inbound processes, repacking, outbound delivery processes, as well as posting changes and stock transfer. Learn more about Unified Package Building in EWM and Unified Package Building via SAP Help.

As we gradually introduced new features, you can find an good overview on which process is supported by UPB via SAP Note information 3076865 - Unified Package Building: Additional Application-Specific Information.

While using the Unified Package Building (UPB) is scope of basic functions, the usage of Package Building (PB) in EWM is scope of advanced functions.

When to use the Package Building Optimizer (PBO)?

The advantage of PBO is that it can also build optimized pallets/packages for different sized products considering their dimensions and the weight. You do not need any package instructions or packaging specification, it does a kind of 3D Tetris to build the pallets. There are simple (stacking) rules available, but you can also configure it in more detail. In addition, the results can be visualized in a 3D scene (transaction /SCMB/TEST_UBP).

How and where to install the SCM Optimizer?

If you are an on-premise customer, you can install the SCM Optimizer on a separate instance (recommended) or on the application service of the consumer solution (e.g. EWM, TM). However, please review SAP Note 1640509 - FAQ about the setup of SCM Optimizer highlighting the advantages and considerations guiding you on this decision.

Please as well review the installation guide mentioned via SAP Note 1686826 - Installation help for installing SCM Optimizers.

If you are a Private Cloud customer, and you have not yet used SCM Optimizer in e.g. your TM PCE system, our Enterprise Cloud Services (ECS) team will install the optimizer on your cloud infrastructure. You can contact your local ECS team and create service requests.

Do I have to perform a “SCM Optimizer migration” when I am transforming from Business Suite to SAP S/4HANA Private Cloud Edition (PCE)?

No, migration activities are not required. The SCM Optimizer is a state-les solution, that does not have an own database or configuration-layer. So, it just can be installed in a greenfield approach during transformation projects. Please ensure that you install the latest optimizer patch available on service marketplace.

As a background, the SCM Optimizer is called from the backend solution with a configuration that is defined in the application layer (e.g. EWM, TM, etc.). This means that there is no migration of SCM Optimizer relevant, when moving from Business Suite (e.g. SAP EWM 9.5) to EWM in SAP S/4HANA (PCE).

What are the commercial aspects to consider using SCM Optimizer in EWM for SAP S/4HANA Private Cloud Edition (PCE)?

In general the usage of Package Building including Package Building Optimizer in EWM is considered advanced scope (included in the advanced license).

If you are a PCE customer, please note that the infrastructure for the SCM Optimizer is not yet included in the EWM subscription. You could consider adding an additional SAP Connection Package for SCM Optimizer hosting.

In case you already have a subscription for Transportation Management for SAP S/4HANA, etc., the included optimizer ca be used by EWM too.

entry.title: SAP EWM: Wave Management (Configuration & Execution)
entry.content:
What is SAP EWM Wave Management?

Wave Management in SAP EWM is a process that allows warehouses to group and manage picking tasks based on specific criteria such as order priority, product availability, and resource allocation. By organizing pick tasks into waves, warehouses can optimize labor resources, improve order fulfillment times, and enhance overall operational efficiency.

Key Benefits of Wave Management

  1. Improved Efficiency: Grouping orders into waves allows for more efficient picking processes and reduces travel time within the warehouse.
  2. Enhanced Visibility: Wave Management provides real-time visibility into order processing, allowing managers to monitor progress and make informed decisions.
  3. Optimized Resource Allocation: By analyzing wave data, organizations can allocate labor and equipment resources more effectively.
  4. Increased Order Accuracy: Wave picking helps reduce errors by enabling pickers to focus on a specific group of orders, minimizing distractions.

Configuration of SAP EWM Wave Management

Define Warehouse Process Type

You can create a separate warehouse process type to manage wave as I have created Warehouse Process Type “OBWA ” for this example.

SPRO > SCM Extended Warehouse Management > Extended Warehouse Management > Cross-Process Settings > Warehouse Task > Define Warehouse Process Type

Select process category “2” as we are creating this process type for stock removal.

Define Control Indicators for Determining Warehouse Process Types

Define determination rule for new created OBWA Warehouse Process Type and Control Indicator “04” for Outbound deliveries.

Determine Storage Type Search Sequence for Stock Removal

For new Warehouse Process Type OBWA, configured to determine Storage Type Search Sequence based on Stock Removal Control + Warehouse Process Type + Stock Type Search Sequence + Stock Removal Rule.

SPRO > SCM Extended Warehouse Management > Extended Warehouse Management > Goods Issue Process > Strategies > Determine Storage Type Search Sequence for Stock Removal

Maintain Wave Types

Create wave types according to your requirements, for this example I have created two wave types

SPRO > SCM Extended Warehouse Management > Extended Warehouse Management > Goods Issue Process > Wave Management > General Settings > Maintain Wave Types

Maintain Wave Categories

Create wave categories according to your requirements, for this example I have created two wave categories

SPRO > SCM Extended Warehouse Management > Extended Warehouse Management > Goods Issue Process > Wave Management > General Settings > Maintain Wave Categories

Set Automatic Wave Generation for Warehouse Process Type

Select “Auto Wave Creation” check for Warehouse Process Type OBWA

SPRO > SCM Extended Warehouse Management > Extended Warehouse Management > Goods Issue Process > Wave Management > General Settings > Set Automatic Wave Generation for Warehouse Process Type

Define Condition Tables

Define condition table for wave configuration including the fields that you are planning to use for waves, for this example I have created table ZN81SHIP and included WME_CUSTOMER, WME_LGNUM, WME_PROCTY fields

SPRO > SCM Extended Warehouse Management > Extended Warehouse Management > Goods Issue Process > Wave Management > Wave Template Determination >Define Condition Tables

Define Access Sequences

SPRO > SCM Extended Warehouse Management > Extended Warehouse Management > Goods Issue Process > Wave Management > Wave Template Determination > Define Access Sequences

Define Condition Types

SPRO > SCM Extended Warehouse Management > Extended Warehouse Management > Goods Issue Process > Wave Management > Wave Template Determination > Define Condition Types

Define Determination Procedure

SPRO > SCM Extended Warehouse Management > Extended Warehouse Management > Goods Issue Process > Wave Management > Wave Template Determination > Define Determination Procedure

Assign Procedure to Document Type

SPRO > SCM Extended Warehouse Management > Extended Warehouse Management > Goods Issue Process > Wave Management > Wave Template Determination > Assign Procedure to Document Type

Define Condition Maintenance Group

Add new created condition type and tables in “WME” Maintenance Groups with Application “WME”, and Usage “WD”

Maintain Wave Templates

Use transaction /SCWM/WAVETMP to create Wave Template(s) using new created wave types & wave categories for Manual & Automatic waves as per your requirements.

Make sure you maintain at least below time attributes for cutoff & release time: Cutoff Time, Release Time, WaveCompletion, and Wave Calendar

Wave Determination Group Condition Maintenance

Use transaction /SCWM/WDGCM to maintain conditions for wave determination

Define Sort Rules for Warehouse Tasks

Defined Sort Rule “PIPA” and assigned Sort Fields “PATHSEQ” Sort Sequence

SPRO > SCM Extended Warehouse Management > Extended Warehouse Management > Cross-Process Settings > Warehouse Order > Define Sort Rules for Warehouse Tasks

Define Filters for Warehouse Order Creation Rules

Created 3 Filters

FLT1 — Weight between 1- 10 LB for Wave Cat C1
FLT2 — Weight upto 10 LB for Wave Cat C2
FLT3 — Weight between 11- 100 kg for Wave Cat C2

SPRO > SCM Extended Warehouse Management > Extended Warehouse Management > Cross-Process Settings > Warehouse Order > Define Filters for Warehouse Order Creation Rules

Define Limit Values for the Size of a Warehouse Order

Defined below limits:
LMT1 Max 1 task in one WO
LMT2 Max 5 task in one WO

SPRO > SCM Extended Warehouse Management > Extended Warehouse Management > Cross-Process Settings > Warehouse Order > Define Limit Values for the Size of a Warehouse Order

Define Packing Profile for Warehouse Order Creation

Create profile(s) and assign your Sort Rule “PIPA”, and select auto HU & WT creation

SPRO > SCM Extended Warehouse Management > Extended Warehouse Management > Cross-Process Settings > Warehouse Order > Define Packing Profile for Warehouse Order Creation

Define Creation Rules for Warehouse Orders

Create WOCR and link with new created Filter, Limit, Sorting and Packaging Profile

WOC1 WOCR — Outbound — 1
WOC2 WOCR — Outbound — 2
WOC3 WOCR — Outbound — 3

SPRO > SCM Extended Warehouse Management > Extended Warehouse Management > Cross-Process Settings > Warehouse Order > Define Creation Rules for Warehouse Orders

Define Search Sequence of Creation Rules for Activity Areas

Created Sort Sequence for new WOCR & Activity Area T210

N810 T210 PICK 1 WOC1 WOCR — Outbound — 1
N810 T210 PICK 2 WOC2 WOCR — Outbound — 2
N810 T210 PICK 3 WOC3 WOCR — Outbound — 3

SPRO > SCM Extended Warehouse Management > Extended Warehouse Management > Cross-Process Settings > Warehouse Order > Define Search Sequence of Creation Rules for Activity Areas

Define Warehouse Process Type

Created a Warehouse Process Type “PIHU” for Picking HU

SPRO > SCM Extended Warehouse Management > Extended Warehouse Management > Cross-Process Settings > Warehouse Task > Define Warehouse Process Type

Define Warehouse Number Control

Added PIHU in Warehouse Number Control under field “Warehouse Process Type for Pick-HU WT for Current Resource” and selected Pick-HU Procedure as “0BDL”

SPRO > SCM Extended Warehouse Management > Extended Warehouse Management > Cross-Process Settings > Warehouse Task > Define Control Indicators for Determining Warehouse Process Types

Assign this Control Indicator to your product

Create PackSpec 

Created new PackSpec for Outbound Wave

Define Activity Areas

Created new general purpose activity area for Storage Type we rare planning to use

Assign Storage Bins to Activity Areas

Define Sort Sequence for Activity Areas

Create virtual bin for activity 

Use transaction /SCWM/LS01 to create new Bin VIRTUAL_PACKBIN_01 in your storage type T210

Maintain Additional Attributes for activity area

Use transaction /SCWM/SEBA and map new Bin VIRTUAL_PACKBIN_01 to Pick Activity in Activity Area T210

Create Sales Order

Create Outbound Delivery

Verify in monitor that Wave is created according to the configurations and new outbound delivery is assigned to new created wave

Also can go to PPF actions of Outbound Delivery and check logs tab under Action Details for step by step logs

Simulate the wave release

From Monitor Outbound > Waves select wave and click Simulate Single wave release from actions button menu to review warehouse tasks and steps that will be created after it’s release

Release the Wave

You can release wave from simulate screen or from wave action toolbar button. 

Once wave is released warehouse task gets created according to the configurations we made.

Monitor and Analyze Waves

Monitoring wave performance is crucial for ongoing optimization. SAP EWM provides various tools for tracking wave progress and analyzing performance metrics.

Conclusion

SAP EWM Wave Management is a powerful tool that can significantly enhance warehouse efficiency and order fulfillment accuracy. By following the steps outlined in this guide, organizations can configure and implement wave management effectively, leading to improved operational performance.

Wave Management allows businesses to adapt to changing demands and optimize their resources, ensuring that they remain competitive in today’s fast-paced environment. As companies continue to evolve and grow, mastering Wave Management in SAP EWM will be essential for achieving operational excellence and meeting customer expectations.

In summary, by leveraging the capabilities of SAP EWM Wave Management, organizations can streamline their warehouse operations, reduce costs, and ultimately deliver better service to their customers.

entry.title: Automatic Replenishment in SAP EWM on SAP S/4HANA 2023
entry.content:
In today's competitive supply chain landscape, operational efficiency and inventory accuracy are crucial to meeting customer expectations and reducing fulfillment costs. Automatic Replenishment in SAP EWM is a strategic tool that helps businesses maintain seamless warehouse operations by ensuring that picking locations are consistently stocked without manual intervention. By automating the movement of goods from reserve areas to fixed picking bins based on real-time stock levels, businesses can prevent order delays, optimize labor utilization, and reduce stockouts. In this blog, we’ll explore how automatic replenishment supports warehouse efficiency and demonstrates its functionality through a practical test scenario.

Let’s dive into the key configuration steps that make this process work effortlessly behind the scenes.

Configuration:

1. Activate Replenishment strategies in storage type

Path: SIMG>SCM EWM> EWM>Internal warehouse process> Replenish Control> Activate Replenishment strategies in storage type

2. Maintain Document types and item types for warehouse request

Path: SIMG>SCM EWM> EWM>Internal warehouse process> Replenish Control> Maintain Document/item Categories for replenishment

3.  Assign Fixed Bins to the products

T. Code : /N/SCWM/BINMAT

4. Stock Removal Strategies

4.1 Specify Storage type Search sequence for Stock removal

Path: SIMG>SCM EWM> EWM >Goods Issue Process>Strategies > Specify Storage type  search sequence for stock removal

4.2 Determine Storage type Search sequence for Stock Removal

Path: SIMG>SCM EWM> EWM >Goods Issue Process>Strategies > Determine Storage type  search sequence for stock removal

5. Warehouse Process Type

5.1 Define Warehouse Process Type

Path: SIMG>SCM EWM> EWM >Cross Process Settings>Warehouse Task >Define Warehouse Process type

5.2 Determine Warehouse Process Type

Path: SIMG>SCM EWM> EWM >Cross Process Settings>Warehouse Task >Determine Warehouse Process type

6. Maintain Product Master

T. Code: /N/SCWM/MAT1

Test Case:

In this test scenario, we will walk through a practical example to validate how Automatic Replenishment works in SAP EWM. The goal is to demonstrate that a Warehouse Task is automatically created when the stock in a bin drop below the predefined minimum quantity threshold.

We’ll simulate a real-life outbound process, starting from the creation of a Sales Order, triggering a pick from fixed bins, and observing how the system handles replenishment post-picking. Verify that the system automatically triggers replenishment when the stock in a fixed bin falls below the minimum quantity after a picking confirmation.

Pre-requisites:
1. Ensure Sufficient Stock is Maintained in the Reserve Area
Before testing replenishment, verify that adequate stock is available in the Reserve Area (HLR1). This stock will be used by the system to automatically replenish the Fixed Bin once the stock level falls below the defined minimum threshold. The replenishment warehouse task will move stock from the reserve area to the fixed bin to ensure seamless picking operations.

2. Ensure Sufficient Stock is Available for Order Picking

Verify that enough stock is available in the Fixed Bin to fulfill the outbound delivery. Once the picking is executed and confirmed, the system checks if the remaining quantity in the bin has fallen below the configured minimum quantity. If so, the follow-up replenishment process is automatically triggered, creating a warehouse task to replenish the fixed bin from the reserve area ensuring readiness for future picking demands.

3. Stock Removal Strategies are Preconfigured for Order Picking

As a prerequisite, ensure that appropriate Stock Removal Strategies are already configured in the system. These strategies determine how the system selects stock from storage bins during the picking process for outbound deliveries. Having this in place ensures that the system can efficiently identify and pick stock from the correct bins when processing the sales order.

Create a Sales Order (T-Code: VA01)

Sales Order is created with a quantity of 26 units. Each fixed bin holds 10 units, and the minimum quantity is configured as 5 units (as shown in Step 3). After the picking process, we expect the system to identify that the stock has dropped below the minimum threshold and automatically trigger a replenishment task from the reserve area.

Create an Outbound Delivery in S/4HANA and Distribute to EWM (T-Code: VL01N)

Generate the outbound delivery based on the sales order and distribute it to the EWM system.

Process the Outbound Delivery Order in EWM (T-Code: /N/SCWM/PRDO)

Access the outbound delivery order and create the picking warehouse tasks.

Create Warehouse Task:
 Outbound Delivery > Follow-on Functions > Warehouse Task

Three Warehouse Tasks have been Successfully Created for Picking. As expected, the system generated three warehouse tasks one for each fixed bin, each containing 10 units, to fulfill the 26-unit Sales Order. Upon confirmation of these tasks, the stock level in each bin is evaluated. Since the remaining quantity falls below the defined minimum threshold, the system is expected to automatically trigger three replenishment tasks from the reserve area.

Let’s now verify these replenishment tasks in the Warehouse Monitor. à Filter with Open Warehouse Task and Whse Process Type - 3010

Immediate Replenishment tasks have been automatically Created. The system has instantly generated replenishment tasks following the confirmation of picking tasks. The automatic creation of replenishment warehouse tasks is a result of the key configuration settings completed earlier such as activating automatic replenishment, defining the appropriate process type, and setting up effective stock removal strategies (as outlined in Steps 1, 4, and 5). These configurations work together to ensure seamless replenishment when fixed bin stock falls below the minimum threshold.

Before proceeding with the confirmation of these replenishment tasks, let’s first review the current stock levels in the fixed bins for the relevant products. This will help us validate that the system correctly identified the stock falling below the minimum quantity threshold and responded as expected.

Stock Status Before Replenishment Confirmation
One bin shows 4 units remaining, while the other two bins have disappeared from available stock, likely due to full depletion. Let’s confirm the replenishment tasks and verify the updated stock levels in the fixed bins.

Post Replenishment Task Confirmation
After confirming the replenishment tasks, all fixed bins are fully restocked and now ready for picking in upcoming outbound processes, as illustrated in the image below.

This example demonstrates how Automatic Replenishment functions in SAP EWM. When stock levels in the picking area fall below the defined minimum quantity, the system proactively creates replenishment tasks to transfer stock from the reserve area to the fixed bins. This ensures that stock is consistently available for picking, supporting uninterrupted outbound operations and enhancing overall warehouse efficiency. It's a powerful feature that helps businesses maintain optimal stock levels and streamline their fulfillment processes.

entry.title: SAP EWM: Pallet Storage Strategy by HU Type
entry.content:
SAP EWM: Pallet Storage Strategy by HU Type

In the realm of warehouse management, effective storage strategies are vital for maximizing space utilization, enhancing operational efficiency, and improving inventory accuracy. SAP Extended Warehouse Management (EWM) offers a robust framework for managing various storage strategies, one of which is the Pallet Storage Strategy by Handling Unit (HU) Type. This article delves into the intricacies of this storage strategy, detailing end-to-end configurations and best practices for implementation.

Understanding Pallet Storage Strategy by Handling Unit Type

What is a Handling Unit?

In SAP EWM, a Handling Unit (HU) refers to a physical unit of goods that can be managed as a single entity. This could be a pallet, carton, or any other container that holds items. The Pallet Storage Strategy by HU Type allows warehouses to define how these handling units are stored based on their characteristics, such as size, weight, or type of goods.

Why Use a Pallet Storage Strategy?

Implementing a Pallet Storage Strategy by HU Type enables organizations to:

  • Maximize Space Utilization: Different handling units can be stored according to their specific requirements, optimizing the use of available space.
  • Enhance Picking Efficiency: By defining storage strategies based on HU types, warehouses can streamline picking processes, reducing travel time for workers.
  • Improve Inventory Accuracy: Properly managed handling units minimize errors in stock management, leading to better inventory control.

End-to-End Configuration of SAP EWM Pallet Storage Strategy by HU Type

Define Storage Types

For this example created new storage type T070 for Pallet Storage. Selected Storage Behavior as“1”, HU Requirement mandatory, additional checks needs to configured HU type check , Put away rule — 5 (Empty bin value). You may also refer SAP standard storage type 0070 for basic configurations.

Define Bin Sections

Created below 2 type of bins sections for small and large types.

SPRO > SCM Extended Warehouse Management > Extended Warehouse Management > Goods Receipt Process > Strategies > Putaway Rules > Storage Behavior: Pallets > Define Bin Sections

Perform Bin Sectioning for Bin Type and HU Type

Mapped existing HU types with new bin sections we created as required.

SPRO > SCM Extended Warehouse Management > Extended Warehouse Management > Goods Receipt Process > Strategies > Putaway Rules > Storage Behavior: Pallets > Perform Bin Sectioning for Bin Type and HU Type

Assign Storage bin types to storage types

Assign created Bin Types to storage type T070 we created for this example.

SPRO > SCM Extended Warehouse Management > Extended Warehouse Management > Goods Receipt Process > Strategies > Storage Bin Determination > Assign Storage bin types to storage types

Define HU Types for each storage type

Assign all our HU types to storage type T070 we created for this example

SPRO > SCM Extended Warehouse Management > Extended Warehouse Management > Goods Receipt Process > Slotting > Influencing Parameters > HU Types > Define HU Types for Each Storage Type

Define HU Types for each storage bin type

Map your HU types to storage type T070 and bin types we created.

SPRO > SCM Extended Warehouse Management > Extended Warehouse Management > Goods Receipt Process > Strategies > Storage Bin Determination > HU Types > Define HU Types for Each Storage Bin Type

Generate Activity Area from Storage Type

Generated for warehouse N810 & T070 that we created for this example

SPRO > SCM Extended Warehouse Management > Extended Warehouse Management > Master Data > Activity Areas > Generate Activity Area from Storage Type

Define Storage Bin Structure 

Defined Storage Bin Structure (for ASL- Aisle/Stack/Level) as below

SPRO > SCM Extended Warehouse Management > Extended Warehouse Management > Master Data > Storage Bins > Define Storage Bin Structure

Create Bins

Created 20 bins as per above structure and then using /SCWM/LS11 edited 10 bins for Type = SMAL

Perform Bin Sorting

Sorted bins using T-code /SCWM/SBST

Define Storage Type Search sequence for putaway

Configured Storage Type Search sequence for putaway in Storage Type T070

SPRO > SCM Extended Warehouse Management > Extended Warehouse Management > Goods Receipt Process > Slotting > Master Data > Storage Type Sequences > Define Storage Type Search Sequence for Putaway

Assign storage types to storage type search sequence

Assigned search sequence to storage type as below

SPRO > SCM Extended Warehouse Management > Extended Warehouse Management > Goods Receipt Process > Strategies > Storage Type Search > Assign Storage Types to Storage Type Search Sequence

Define putaway control indicator

Defined putaway control P070 for new search sequence. (Use this in your product)

SPRO > SCM Extended Warehouse Management > Extended Warehouse Management > Goods Receipt Process > Slotting > Master Data > Define Putaway Control Indicator

Specify Storage Type Search sequence for putaway

Mapped Storage Type Search sequence as below

SPRO > SCM Extended Warehouse Management > Extended Warehouse Management > Goods Receipt Process > Strategies > Storage Type Search > Specify Storage Type Search sequence for putaway

Optimize access strategy for storage type search: putaway

SPRO > SCM Extended Warehouse Management > Extended Warehouse Management > Goods Receipt Process > Strategies > Storage Type Search > Optimize Access Strategy for Storage Type Search: Putaway

Test your configurations

To test this process created a purchase order for product that has our new putaway control P070 assigned and inbound delivery with pallet HUs. Created HU tasks for our distributed delivery and reviewed the destination bins in our warehouse tasks. As you can notice in below image that Pallet Storage Sections are used according to the configurations we made in this example.

Conclusion

Implementing a pallet storage strategy by HU type in SAP EWM is essential for optimizing warehouse operations. By configuring handling unit types, storage types, and storage strategies, organizations can significantly enhance inventory management efficiency. This approach not only maximizes space utilization but also improves the accuracy and speed of warehouse processes. As businesses continue to evolve and face new challenges in supply chain management, leveraging advanced functionalities like HU-based storage strategies will be key to maintaining competitive advantages in a dynamic market. Proper planning, configuration, and testing are critical to the successful deployment of these strategies, ultimately leading to smoother operations and improved customer satisfaction.

entry.title: New features in SAP Warehouse Operator Version 2.5
entry.content:
New features in SAP Warehouse Operator Version 2.5

We have just released SAP Warehouse Operator Version 2.5 to the Apple App Store. Let’s have a look together at the new key capabilities:

  • Ad Hoc product warehouse task creation
  • Picking without Pick Handling Units

 

Ad Hoc Product Warehouse Task Creation

Ad hoc movements are unplanned goods movements that you create manually within a warehouse. The decision to make an ad hoc movement is not controlled by the system but by the warehouse operator. There can be several reasons for it, like wrong products located in a storage bin, damaged products or unplanned rearrangements of goods. It’s a great use case to be supported on mobile devices. We have now added ad hoc product warehouse task creation to the existing functionality to create warehouse tasks for the movement of Handling Units.

 

 

 

Picking without Pick Handling Units

You may now also use the app to pick items without a Pick Handling Unit. Simply create warehouse tasks for picking in the backend without assigning a Pick Handling Unit. These warehouse tasks will then also show up in the SAP Warehouse Operator app on your iPhone and can be started as well.

 

Try the app and engage with us

Please download the new version of the app and try it out yourself.

Please leave a comment below if you have questions or feedback and we’ll get back to you.

You may also contact us at SAP_Warehouse_Operator@sap.com if you have questions on the configuration to use the app, the setup process or have specific requirements.

We also encourage you to visit our SAP Mobile Applications community page and click “follow”. We’ll be publishing more informative blog posts. Please post questions in the SAP Extended Warehouse Management area. Want to be notified? Check your profile settings to ensure you have your settings activated

entry.title: Exception Handling in SAP EWM
entry.content:
One of the critical aspects of SAP EWM system is its ability to handle exceptions. Exception handling in SAP EWM allows businesses to manage unexpected situations efficiently and ensure smooth warehouse operations. In this blog, we will explore how SAP EWM handles exceptions, the types of exceptions you may encounter, and the essential steps to configure and manage them effectively.

What are Exceptions & Exception Codes in SAP EWM?

In SAP Extended Warehouse Management (EWM), “exceptions” refer to situations that deviate from the normal warehouse operations, like damaged goods, incorrect quantities, or system errors, which are managed and resolved through a system of exception codes, allowing warehouse staff to handle these inconsistencies during processes like putaway or picking and update the system accordingly.

Steps to Configure and Handle Exceptions in SAP EWM

As we know SAP EWM Exception Handling works based on Business Context and Exception Step in combination of configured Exception Code profile and Internal Process code

For this example we will consider one inbound scenario where during putaway process you need to use different bin due to some issue with existing bin in the warehouse task. We will not be customizing standard exception codes, and will use standard CHBD exception code here.

Use below IMG path to check standard putaway Business Context “TPT” configuration

SPRO > SCM Extended Warehouse Management > Extended Warehouse Management > Cross-Process Settings > Exception Handling > Maintain Business Context for Exception Codes

Ensure Operation/Execution Step “04” for RF HU Whse task Action is assigned to Business Context “TPT”

In this example we will be using Internal Process Code CHBD so check it is assigned to Business Context “TPT” & Execution Step “04”

Use below IMG path to ensure Exception Code CHBD that we are planning to use is configured for your warehouse

SPRO > SCM Extended Warehouse Management > Extended Warehouse Management > Cross-Process Settings > Exception Handling > Define Exception Codes

And assigned to assigned to Business Context “TPT” & Execution Step “04” for our warehouse

Test your exception handling configurations

For this example created a Purchase Order and Inbound Delivery 180018139

Created HU Warehouse Tasks to create test data to test our exception handling configuration as you may have noticed this warehouse order has destination bin “IOWA-01–01–05” and we will use different destination bin “IOWA-01–01–07” using the Exception Code CHBD

Login to RF UI using transaction /SCWM/RFUI and navigate to Putaway by WO screen and enter above warehouse Order

Enter different destination bin “IOWA-01–01–07” and press enter => Enter Exception Code CHBD on error screen => Re-enter destination bin “IOWA-01–01–07” => and finally confirm the destination bin on last screen to confirm the warehouse order.

Now go back to your warehouse order and verify the new destination bin “IOWA-01–01–07” is updated and Exception Code CHBD is also reflecting as expected.

Conclusion

Effective exception handling in SAP EWM is vital for maintaining the integrity of warehouse operations and ensuring customer satisfaction. By implementing structured configurations such as defining exception types, setting handling rules, and integrating processes, organizations can proactively manage disruptions and minimize their impact on operations. The ability to monitor and report on exceptions not only aids in immediate resolution but also supports continuous improvement efforts. As supply chain complexities grow, mastering exception handling in SAP EWM will empower businesses to navigate challenges with agility and resilience, fostering a culture of operational excellence.

entry.title: The SAP Extended Warehouse Management FAQ Series: Release Strategy for Warehouse Management
entry.content:
SAP has a long-standing commitment to supporting our clients in managing their warehouse operations with outstanding adaptability and efficiency covering simple to complex requirements across industries. With LE-WM, Stock Room Management, SAP Extended Warehouse Management (EWM), EWM for SAP S/4HANA, SAP Warehouse Management in SAP S/4HANA Cloud we are continuously evolving our solution portfolio to offer the right solution for every marketsegment.  

With this Blog post we would like to give an insight on the most relevant questions when it comes to release strategy, important milestones and transformation project planning for SAP’s warehouse management solutions.

What is the release strategy for EWM for SAP S/4HANA?

We are continuously enhancing EWM for SAP S/4HANA with a clear focus on the following main themes:

  • Embedded Artificial Intelligence features to increase warehouse efficiency, data-driven decision making, simplify user-experience
  • Suite integration and modularization enabling a deep integration of warehousing processes to manufacturing and production, transportation management, order management and logistics network solutions
  • Continuous extension of features and functions addressing industry needs

With Extended Warehouse Management as an integral component of SAP S/4HANA Private Cloud and onPremise we are shipping a major release in a two-year cycle (since SAP S/4HANA 2023). Additionally, we release in-between major releases, three feature packs with more innovations and features along our strategic north-star.

Learn more about the SAP S/4HANA release and maintenance strategy via SAP News Center - New SAP S/4HANA Release and Maintenance Strategy to Deliver Greater Innovation and Flexibility and SAP Maintenance Phases.

We recommend reviewing the SAP Road Map Explorer to learn more about the specific Future Features and innovations we are planning with upcoming SAP S/4HANA Supply Chain for Extended Warehouse Management releases.

What release milestones are relevant for planning the transformation of SAP EWM?

As a predecessor to EWM for SAP S/4HANA, SAP EWM 9.5, 9.4 and earlier releases, are highly productive at many of our customers. We recommend to check the specific maintenance plan for SAP EWM releases via the SAP Product Availability Matrix to understand, the important timelines of end of mainstream maintenance and to understand if there is extended maintenance available.

It is important to note that not all product versions have an availability of extended maintenance and have planned end of mainstream maintenance in 2025 (e.g. SAP EWM 9.4). Learn more about the maintenance timelines of SAP EWM via SAP Note 2911449 - Updated Maintenance Timelines for SAP EWM Product Versions.

If you are evaluating transformation options, we recommend the following SAP Blog Posts for further information - Transformation to SAP S/4HANA for Warehouse and Transportation Management and SAP Note 2976757 - SAP S/4HANA Migration Cockpit: Central Note for Transfer Data Directly from SAP System - SAP EWM to EWM based on SAP S/4HANA.

What is the difference between LE-WM and Stock Room Management?

For our customers who are currently operating their warehouses with LE-WM, the following aspects are important to consider as transformation strategy boundary conditions:

  • LE-WM running on SAP ERP - please review the maintenance plan for your operative SAP ERP release via the SAP Product Availability Matrix.
  • LE-WM running on SAP S/4HANA - LE-WM is running in the compatibility scope with restricted usage rights, that expire according to general Compatibility Scope guidelines. Please review SAP Note 2269324 - Compatibility Scope Matrix for SAP S/4HANA for further information.
  • LE-WM running on SAP S/4HANA under RISE agreement there is an extended restricted usage rights period defined. Please review SAP Note 2269324 - Compatibility Scope Matrix for SAP S/4HANA for further information.

Stock Room Management is offering a subset of LE-WM functionalities. The offering is intended to enable our installed base customers to continue running their legacy  Warehouse Management installations in SAP S/4HANA beyond the timelines of the compatibility scope.

Please review the following SAP Notes to learn more about Stock Room Management:

  • SAP Note 2881166 - FAQ : Stock Room Management
  • SAP Note 2882809 - Scope Compliance Check for Stock Room Management
  • SAP Note 2577428 - Roadmap for LE-WM in S/4HANA

We would like to recommend the following SAP Blogs for further information around the topic of strategy, product versions and more:

  • SAP EWM Podcast - Episode 01 - History of SAP Extended Warehouse Management
  • Transformation to SAP S/4HANA for Warehouse and Transportation Management
  • Compatibility Scope: What Happens after 2025/2030
